featured.title: Making Peace with LLM Non-determinism
featured.description:
Non-determinism in LLMs has always bothered me. So I decided to understand it and make peace with it. 

I wandered through all the sources I could find:

- Sampling
- Random Seeding
- Sparse MoE and batched inference
- GPU operation orders
- Chip’s orientation to the sky…

…just to realize that my grievances weren't about non-determinism, but about language itself. 

Special thanks to Julian Richey, a jazz guitarist and rock climber who designs chips for Amazon, for his invaluable insights on hardware non-determinism.

Blog: https://lnkd.in/eh-fTfAC

featured.title: Our Humble Attempt at “How Much Data Do You Need to Fine-Tune”
featured.description:
"How much data do you need to fine-tune" has been a lingering question for me and my friends. We had vague intuitions that the answer would be task-dependent and probably in the hundreds, but couldn't substantiate those claims. 

Recently, we decided to run some quick and practical research on the openAI fine-tuning API to begin answering this question for us and the community. 

In the experiment, we: 
- Saw significant improvements on two practical tasks with only ~100 data point
- Found an unintuitive 3.6X faster inference speed on the fine-tuning models
- Observed some interesting non-divergence and catastrophic forgetting
- Got...roasted hard by LLMs

Here's our results published on substack: https://lnkd.in/e6vt8UBK

Done with my friends who think and talk about LLMs on a daily basis: Daniel Chang Emma Qian Michael Agaby

Their substacks:
https://lnkd.in/ejAJfnWw
https://lnkd.in/e9AQ-d_g
https://lnkd.in/ehmKGQsZ

featured.title: Barry’s LLM Product Field Notes
featured.description:
I've had a lot of fun building LLM products in the past couple of months, and I want to share my experiences. This is an opinionated collection of my learnings on topics that I have thought deeply about: 
- personalization
- agents
- benchmarking
- retrieval

I hope some of these might help you in your own journey, while others may provoke some debates.

Again, open-access notion doc:

Does prompt length affect OpenAI API latency? A quick experiment

Long story short, probably not.

API latency has a pretty big impact on user experiences for LLM applications and it seemed plausible that input length might affect the inference speed and therefore latency. So I ran a quick experiment on GPT-3.5 to find out.

The measurements are not the most precise, but at the scale where it matters, it seems like:

- Total latency is almost entirely affected by output length is independent of input length 
- First-token generation latency is also almost stochastic w.r.t input length

My guess is that this is due to the fixed-length encoding where anything within the token limit are treated the same way. Curious to hear others' theories.

experiment detail in this doc: https://lnkd.in/epWKTqtY

One-line distillation from GPT-4 to GPT-3.5

Wrote a smol library for one-line model distillation over the weekend. Still experimenting with task granularity and hyperparameters to make it useful but sharing the code for whoever might want to run similar experiments.

Give it a list of prompts, it will:
- generate responses from GPT-4
- upload the data to openAI
- fine-tune a GPT-3.5 model

Only made these following parameters configurable:
- GPT-4 system prompt, temperature, max_tokens
- Fine-tuning n_epoches and n_data_repetition

Adding data augmentation and cost estimation in the coming days.

https://lnkd.in/ep9mPGaQ
